name: Apple Vision Pro
short_description: A head-worn spatial computer that blends digital content with the physical world and is controlled by eyes, hands, and voice.
long_description: Apple Vision Pro is a mixed-reality headset Apple markets as a 'spatial computer', pairing dual micro-OLED displays with an array of cameras and sensors and custom M2 and R1 silicon to overlay app windows and 3D content onto a passthrough view of the room. Announced at WWDC in June 2023 and released in the US on February 2, 2024, it is driven by visionOS and steered through eye tracking, hand gestures, and voice rather than controllers. For physical AI in daily life it is a notable case of real-time, head-worn perception: the device continuously senses the wearer's surroundings, gaze, and hands to fuse computation with the lived environment, foreshadowing always-on situated assistants.

images.caption: Apple Vision Pro headset resting on a surface, front three-quarter view.
images.alt_text: An Apple Vision Pro headset with a curved glass front and aluminum frame, photographed at an angle on a light surface.

sources.title: Apple Vision Pro available in the U.S. on February 2
sources.access_notes: Apple Newsroom press release confirming US release date.

sources.title: Apple Vision Pro
sources.access_notes: Background on hardware (M2/R1 silicon, micro-OLED displays) and visionOS.

interactions.title: Drag your Mac's screen into the air
interactions.description: Glance at your MacBook and a single giant, private 4K display floats out of it into the room — you keep typing on the physical keyboard and trackpad while the window hangs wherever you parked it.

interactions.title: Sit across from someone who isn't there
interactions.description: On a FaceTime call with another headset, each person appears as a life-scale spatial Persona placed in your room, with spatial audio coming from where they're 'sitting' so it feels like you're at the same table.

interactions.title: Step back inside a memory
interactions.description: Open a spatial video your iPhone captured and the moment unfolds around you in depth — a kid's birthday, a dog mid-leap — close enough that you instinctively lean in to look around it.
interactions.source_label: Apple Support — spatial photos and videos

interactions.title: Surgeon's heads-up display in the OR
interactions.description: A surgeon performed cataract surgery wearing Vision Pro running a custom app (ScopeXR), seeing the microscope feed in stereoscopic 3D with preoperative scans overlaid, all without breaking sterile technique.

interactions.title: Wearing it behind the wheel
interactions.description: Viral videos showed drivers piloting Teslas while wearing the headset, prompting safety warnings from US officials and a reminder that Apple's own guide says not to use it while operating a vehicle.

interactions.title: Typing on thin air in public
interactions.description: Days after launch, clips of people pinching at invisible windows on the NYC subway and city sidewalks went viral, turning the disembodied hand gestures into a meme about wearing a spatial computer in public.

interactions.title: Pin reference material around a physical task
interactions.description: Repairing something at a workbench, you anchor a wiring diagram and a how-to video to the wall beside the part, then look back and forth between the floating panels and your hands without picking up a phone — using the same passthrough and gaze-and-pinch control the headset already runs.

interactions.title: A quiet label on what you're looking at
interactions.description: Glance at an appliance's controls or a houseplant and ask aloud what it is, getting a spoken answer drawn from the forward cameras' view — grounded in the device's existing vision input, microphones, and voice control, not a shipped feature.
